vignette: A 70-year-old nursing home resident presents with fever, confusion, and hypotension. Vitals: Temp 38.9°C, BP 78/45, HR 115, RR 30. Lactate is 4.5 mmol/L. She has a chronic Foley catheter. What is the FIRST priority in the 1-hour sepsis bundle?

explanation.whyCorrect: Sepsis 1-hour bundle: Fluids 30mL/kg first to restore perfusion. Then blood cultures, antibiotics, vasopressors.

whyWrong.A: Antibiotics are crucial but fluids come first.
whyWrong.B: Blood cultures before antibiotics but not before fluids.
whyWrong.D: Lactate already measured.
whyWrong.E: Central line is not in the 1-hour bundle.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Sepsis: Lactate → Blood cultures → Antibiotics → Fluids 30mL/kg → Vasopressors. Time = Tissue.

vignette: A 25-year-old healthy female presents with dysuria, frequency, and urgency for 2 days. No fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. Urine dipstick: positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Not pregnant. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5 days (first-line).

whyWrong.A: Ceftriaxone IM is for pyelonephritis.
whyWrong.C: Fluoroquinolones should be AVOIDED for uncomplicated cystitis.
whyWrong.D: Empiric treatment without culture is appropriate.
whyWrong.E: TMP-SMX is alternative if resistance <20%.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin > TMP-SMX > Fosfomycin. AVOID fluoroquinolones.

vignette: A 28-year-old male is newly diagnosed with HIV. CD4 count is 180 cells/μL. He is started on TDF/FTC + dolutegravir. Which prophylactic medication should he also receive?

explanation.whyCorrect: CD4 <200 = PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX DS daily.

whyWrong.B: MAC prophylaxis is for CD4 <50.
whyWrong.C: Fluconazole is not routine prophylaxis.
whyWrong.D: Valganciclovir is for CMV in transplant.
whyWrong.E: CD4 <200 requires PCP prophylaxis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HIV prophylaxis: CD4 <200 → TMP-SMX (PCP). CD4 <50 → Add Azithromycin (MAC).

vignette: A 28-year-old IV drug user presents with fever, chills, and night sweats for 2 weeks. He has a new holosystolic murmur at the apex, splinter hemorrhages, and Janeway lesions on his palms. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Echo shows 1.5cm vegetation on the mitral valve. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Right-sided IE in IVDU: MSSA = Nafcillin/Oxacillin. Add Gentamicin for first 3-5 days for synergy. Duration: 6 weeks. Vancomycin is for MRSA or penicillin-allergic patients.

whyWrong.A: Vancomycin is for MRSA. Patient has MSSA.
whyWrong.C: Vancomycin is inferior to beta-lactams for MSSA.
whyWrong.D: Ceftriaxone is for HACEK organisms.
whyWrong.E: Surgery indicated if: Heart failure, abscess, emboli, fungal, or >10mm vegetation with emboli.

explanation.clinicalPearl: MSSA IE: Nafcillin/Oxacillin x6 weeks. MRSA IE: Vancomycin or Daptomycin x6 weeks. Early surgery if complications.

vignette: A 55-year-old diabetic male presents with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot for 4 weeks. He can probe to bone. MRI shows osteomyelitis of the 1st metatarsal. What is the MOST important diagnostic test?

options.text: Bone biopsy with culture and histopathology

explanation.whyCorrect: Bone biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of osteomyelitis. Provides microbiological diagnosis and antibiotic sensitivities. Probe-to-bone test + MRI strongly suggest osteomyelitis but biopsy confirms and guides therapy.

whyWrong.A: MRI is excellent for diagnosis but does not provide microbiology.
whyWrong.C: CT is less sensitive than MRI for osteomyelitis.
whyWrong.D: Wound swabs correlate poorly with bone pathogens.
whyWrong.E: Blood cultures are positive in only 25-50% of diabetic foot osteomyelitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Osteomyelitis: Bone biopsy is gold standard. Empiric antibiotics ONLY after biopsy. Treat 4-6 weeks based on culture.

vignette: A 68-year-old male develops a UTI while hospitalized. Urine culture grows E. coli resistant to ceftriaxone, cefepime, and ciprofloxacin but sensitive to meropenem and amikacin. What resistance mechanism is MOST likely present?

explanation.whyCorrect: ESBL-producing E. coli: Resistant to cephalosporins (ceftriaxone, cefepime) and fluoroquinolones, but sensitive to carbapenems (meropenem). ESBLs hydrolyze cephalosporins but NOT carbapenems.

whyWrong.A: Methicillin resistance is in Staphylococcus (MRSA), not E. coli.
whyWrong.C: KPC-producing organisms are resistant to carbapenems.
whyWrong.D: Vancomycin resistance is in Enterococci (VRE).
whyWrong.E: AmpC would confer resistance to cephamycins and some cephalosporins.

explanation.clinicalPearl: ESBL: Resistant to cephalosporins + fluoroquinolones, sensitive to carbapenems. Treatment: Meropenem, Ertapenem.

vignette: A 35-year-old HIV patient (CD4 25 cells/μL, not on ART) presents with severe headache, fever, and confusion for 5 days. CSF opening pressure is 35 cmH2O. India ink stain of CSF shows encapsulated yeast. What is the BEST initial treatment?

options.text: Amphotericin B + Flucytosine for 2 weeks, then fluconazole

explanation.whyCorrect: Cryptococcal meningitis in HIV (CD4 <100): Induction: Amphotericin B + Flucytosine x2 weeks. Consolidation: Fluconazole 400-800mg x8 weeks. Maintenance: Fluconazole 200mg until CD4 >100 on ART for >3 months.

whyWrong.B: Fluconazole alone has higher mortality than Amphotericin combination.
whyWrong.C: Voriconazole has poor CSF penetration and is not for cryptococcal meningitis.
whyWrong.D: Echinocandins (caspofungin) have NO activity against Cryptococcus.
whyWrong.E: Amphotericin B monotherapy is inferior to combination with flucytosine.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Cryptococcal meningitis: Amphotericin B + Flucytosine (induction) → Fluconazole (consolidation/maintenance).

vignette: A 30-year-old traveler returns from sub-Saharan Africa with fever, chills, and headache for 3 days. He did not take malaria prophylaxis. Blood smear shows ring forms within RBCs. Parasitemia is 8%. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Severe malaria (parasitemia >5%): IV Artesunate is first-line (superior to quinine). If artesunate unavailable: IV Quinidine or Quinine. Oral treatment is for uncomplicated malaria (parasitemia <5%).

whyWrong.A: Chloroquine resistance is widespread in sub-Saharan Africa.
whyWrong.C: Atovaquone-proguanil is for uncomplicated malaria (oral).
whyWrong.D: Quinine + Doxycycline is alternative if artesunate unavailable.
whyWrong.E: Primaquine is for liver stages (P. vivax/ovale), not acute treatment.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Severe malaria (>5% parasitemia or organ dysfunction): IV Artesunate. Uncomplicated: Artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) or Atovaquone-proguanil.

vignette: A 22-year-old female presents with mucopurulent cervical discharge, dysuria, and intermenstrual bleeding. She has had 3 sexual partners in the past year and uses condoms inconsistently. What is the empiric treatment for the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BID x7 days

explanation.whyCorrect: Mucopurulent cervicitis: Empiric treatment for gonorrhea AND chlamydia. Ceftriaxone IM for gonorrhea + Doxycycline x7 days for chlamydia (preferred over azithromycin due to resistance). Always treat both.

whyWrong.B: Azithromycin alone does not adequately cover gonorrhea.
whyWrong.C: Metronidazole is for trichomoniasis or BV.
whyWrong.D: Azithromycin for chlamydia is less effective than doxycycline.
whyWrong.E: Penicillin is for syphilis, not cervicitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Cervicitis: Treat for GC (Ceftriaxone IM) + Chlamydia (Doxycycline x7d). Doxycycline is preferred over azithromycin for chlamydia.

vignette: A 65-year-old male with COPD and diabetes asks about recommended vaccines. He received influenza vaccine last year and Tdap 3 years ago. He has never received pneumococcal vaccine. What is the BEST recommendation?

explanation.whyCorrect: New 2023 ACIP guidelines: Adults ≥65 years or high-risk (COPD, DM) should receive a SINGLE dose of PCV15 or PCV20. No longer need PPSV23 if PCV20 given. This simplifies the previous recommendation.

whyWrong.A: Outdated recommendation (now replaced by PCV20 alone).
whyWrong.B: Outdated sequential recommendation.
whyWrong.D: PPSV23 alone is less immunogenic than conjugate vaccines.
whyWrong.E: Pneumococcal vaccination is indicated in COPD and DM.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Adults ≥65 or high-risk: PCV20 (or PCV15) ONCE. No PPSV23 needed if PCV20 given.

vignette: A 55-year-old female develops erythema, warmth, and purulent drainage from her abdominal surgical incision 10 days after colectomy. She has a temperature of 38.5°C. What is the MOST important management step?

options.text: CT abdomen to rule out abscess

explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical site infection with purulent drainage: Source control is paramount. Open the wound, drain pus, debride necrotic tissue. Antibiotics are ADJUNCTIVE (not primary) for most SSIs unless there is systemic spread.

whyWrong.A: Antibiotics alone without drainage will fail.
whyWrong.C: CT is indicated if deep infection/abscess suspected, but opening wound comes first.
whyWrong.D: Antibiotics are secondary to source control.
whyWrong.E: Topical antibiotics are insufficient for established infection.

explanation.clinicalPearl: SSI with pus: Drain first, antibiotics second. Source control > Antibiotics in surgical infections.

vignette: A 70-year-old male is admitted with urosepsis. Blood cultures grow E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and TMP-SMX. He has been on IV piperacillin-tazobactam for 3 days and is now afebrile and stable. What is the BEST next step?

options.text: Continue IV piperacillin-tazobactam for 7 more days
Switch to oral TMP-SMX to complete 7 days
Stop antibiotics, blood cultures are negative now

explanation.whyCorrect: Antibiotic de-escalation: Narrow to the most targeted agent based on sensitivities. Switch from IV to PO when clinically stable (afebrile, tolerating PO). TMP-SMX PO is appropriate for susceptible E. coli. Complete 7-10 days total.

whyWrong.A: Continuing broad-spectrum IV therapy promotes resistance and C. diff.
whyWrong.C: Switching to another IV when patient is stable for PO is unnecessary.
whyWrong.D: Urosepsis requires full course (7-10 days), not stopping at 3 days.
whyWrong.E: Fluoroquinolones have black box warnings and should be avoided if alternatives exist.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Antibiotic stewardship: De-escalate to narrowest spectrum. IV→PO switch when stable. Shortest effective duration.

vignette: A 35-year-old farmer presents with fever, headache, myalgia, and conjunctival suffusion. He has jaundice and acute kidney injury. He raises cattle and his farm flooded recently. What is the MOST likely pathogen?

explanation.whyCorrect: Leptospirosis: Contact with animal urine-contaminated water (flooding, farming). Triad: Fever + Conjunctival suffusion + Jaundice/AKI (Weil disease). Treatment: Penicillin or doxycycline. Prevention: Doxycycline prophylaxis for high-risk exposure.

whyWrong.A: Brucellosis: Undulant fever, from unpasteurized dairy or animal contact.
whyWrong.C: Q fever: Pneumonia + Hepatitis, from farm animals (inhalation).
whyWrong.D: Tularemia: Ulceroglandular syndrome, from rabbits/ticks.
whyWrong.E: RMSF: Rash (palms/soles), tick bite, not associated with flooding.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Leptospirosis: Fever + Conjunctival suffusion + Jaundice/AKI + Flooding exposure. Treat with penicillin or doxycycline.

vignette: A 55-year-old female receiving chemotherapy for breast cancer presents with fever (39.0°C) and neutropenia (ANC 200). She has a Port-a-Cath. What is the BEST empiric antibiotic regimen?

explanation.whyCorrect: Febrile neutropenia (high-risk: ANC <100, expected >7 days): Monotherapy with antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime, meropenem, pip-tazo) is first-line. Add vancomycin ONLY if: hemodynamic instability, catheter-related infection, positive blood cultures for gram-positive, skin/soft tissue infection, or known MRSA colonization.

whyWrong.A: Oral antibiotics are for LOW-risk febrile neutropenia only (ANC >100, expected <7 days, stable).
whyWrong.C: Vancomycin is NOT routine. Added only for specific indications.
whyWrong.D: Meropenem is reserved for ESBL risk or recent broad-spectrum antibiotic use.
whyWrong.E: Ceftriaxone lacks Pseudomonas coverage (inadequate for febrile neutropenia).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Febrile neutropenia: Antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime) alone. Add vancomycin only for specific indications.

vignette: A 70-year-old male with diabetes and hypertension is hospitalized with COVID-19 pneumonia. He requires 4L O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%. CRP and D-dimer are elevated. What is the recommended treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Hospitalized COVID-19 requiring supplemental O2: Dexamethasone 6mg daily (reduces mortality in RECOVERY trial) + Remdesivir (antiviral, speeds recovery) + Baricitinib (JAK inhibitor, reduces inflammation if CRP >75). Combination reduces mortality vs dexamethasone alone.

whyWrong.B: Antiviral alone without anti-inflammatory (steroids) is insufficient for severe disease.
whyWrong.C: Steroids alone may be adequate but combination therapy is superior.
whyWrong.D: Hydroxychloroquine + Azithromycin showed NO benefit and potential harm.
whyWrong.E: Convalescent plasma has limited efficacy and is reserved for immunosuppressed patients.

explanation.clinicalPearl: COVID-19 hospitalized on O2: Dexamethasone + Remdesivir ± Baricitinib/Tocilizumab (if elevated inflammatory markers).

vignette: A 70-year-old nursing home resident with central line and recent broad-spectrum antibiotics has persistent candidemia. Blood culture yeast is identified as Candida auris. What is the BEST treatment per CDC/IDSA 2024?

options.text: Echinocandin (micafungin or anidulafungin) + Infection control (contact precautions, chlorhexidine bathing)

explanation.whyCorrect: C. auris is a MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT Candida species. Often resistant to fluconazole (90%), variable resistance to amphotericin B (30%), and some strains resistant to echinocandins. Echinocandins are empiric first-line pending susceptibilities. INFECTION CONTROL CRITICAL: Contact precautions, chlorhexidine bathing, environmental cleaning (C. auris survives on surfaces for weeks). Notify public health. Remove all lines if possible.

whyWrong.A: C. auris is often fluconazole-resistant (90%+); not empiric.
whyWrong.B: Echinocandins + Aggressive infection control. Report to public health.
whyWrong.C: Voriconazole has variable activity; not first-line.
whyWrong.D: Amphotericin B may have resistance; echinocandins preferred.
whyWrong.E: Candidemia always requires antifungal treatment + source control.

explanation.clinicalPearl: C. auris: MDR yeast. Echinocandins first-line. Contact precautions + Environmental cleaning. Report to public health. Survives on surfaces for weeks.

vignette: A 35-year-old male with HIV (CD4 450, suppressed on Biktarvy x3 years) has difficulty with daily pill adherence. What is the FDA-approved long-acting injectable regimen?

explanation.whyCorrect: Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine) is the first FDA-approved long-acting injectable complete ART regimen. ATLAS and FLAIR trials showed non-inferiority to daily oral ART. Initially approved monthly (2021), now approved every 2 months (2022). Requires viral suppression on oral ART first. Oral lead-in optional. Ideal for patients with adherence challenges or preference for fewer doses.

whyWrong.A: Oral ART is the problem (adherence challenges).
whyWrong.B: Cabenuva: IM q2mo. First complete LA-ART regimen. High patient satisfaction.
whyWrong.C: DTG monotherapy not recommended (risk of resistance).
whyWrong.D: Efavirenz is older, more side effects; not long-acting.
whyWrong.E: Boosted PI is older; more metabolic side effects.

explanation.clinicalPearl: LA-ART: Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine) IM q2mo. For suppressed patients. ATLAS/FLAIR trials. Game-changer for adherence.

vignette: A 65-year-old unvaccinated male with diabetes is exposed to COVID-19 (household contact, tested positive today). He has no symptoms. What is the recommended prophylaxis?

options.text: Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir (Paxlovid) OR Remdesivir IV for 3 days OR Molnupiravir

explanation.whyCorrect: High-risk unvaccinated patient with significant exposure: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is recommended per NIH 2024. Options: Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) x5-10 days, Remdesivir IV x3 days, or Molnupiravir (if other options unavailable). Start within 5-7 days of exposure. Also: Test immediately, mask, and monitor symptoms. Vaccination should be initiated after recovery.

whyWrong.A: High-risk unvaccinated patient warrants PEP.
whyWrong.B: Paxlovid or Remdesivir for PEP in high-risk patients.
whyWrong.C: Hydroxychloroquine has NO benefit (multiple RCTs).
whyWrong.D: Ivermectin has NO benefit (multiple RCTs).
whyWrong.E: Convalescent plasma is for treatment of immunocompromised, not PEP.

explanation.clinicalPearl: COVID PEP (2024): Paxlovid or Remdesivir for high-risk exposed patients. Start within 5-7 days. Vaccinate after recovery.

vignette: A 30-year-old immigrant from India has positive IGRA (QuantiFERON) and normal CXR. He is HIV-negative. What is the SHORTEST recommended LTBI treatment per CDC 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: 3HP (rifapentine + INH weekly x12 weeks) is the SHORTEST recommended regimen. PREVENT TB study showed non-inferiority to 9 months INH with higher completion rates (82% vs 69%). 4R (rifampin x4 months) is alternative. 9H is standard but longest duration. All are acceptable; 3HP preferred for adherence. No treatment not recommended (risk of reactivation).

whyWrong.A: 9H is standard but longest (poor adherence).
whyWrong.B: 3HP: Shortest regimen. Weekly DOT. Highest completion rates. Preferred.
whyWrong.C: 4R is 4 months; longer than 3HP.
whyWrong.D: 3HR is less common; 3HP preferred.
whyWrong.E: Positive IGRA with normal CXR = LTBI; treatment recommended to prevent reactivation.

explanation.clinicalPearl: LTBI shortest regimen: 3HP (rifapentine + INH) weekly x12 weeks. PREVENT TB. Highest completion rates (82%).

vignette: A 65-year-old female with pyelonephritis has urine culture growing E. coli resistant to ceftriaxone, cefepime, and ciprofloxacin, but sensitive to meropenem. What is the BEST treatment?

options.text: Ertapenem 1g IV daily OR Meropenem 1g IV q8h

explanation.whyCorrect: ESBL-producing E. coli: Resistant to all cephalosporins and fluoroquinolones. CARBAPENEMS are the treatment of choice. Ertapenem (convenient once-daily, spares anti-pseudomonal carbapenems) for non-severe infections. Meropenem for severe infections (sepsis, ICU). Avoid TMP-SMX (high resistance in ESBL), nitrofurantoin (only for cystitis, not pyelonephritis), and oral fosfomycin (not for pyelonephritis).

whyWrong.A: ESBL hydrolyzes ceftriaxone (resistant).
whyWrong.B: Carbapenems = Treatment of choice for serious ESBL infections.
whyWrong.C: ESBL often co-resistant to fluoroquinolones (resistant here).
whyWrong.D: TMP-SMX often resistant in ESBL; not for pyelonephritis.
whyWrong.E: Nitrofurantoin is for cystitis only (does not achieve renal tissue levels).

explanation.clinicalPearl: ESBL Enterobacteriaceae: Carbapenems (ertapenem or meropenem). Avoid cephalosporins (even if reported susceptible). Fluoroquinolones often co-resistant.

vignette: A 25-year-old MSM presents with a painless genital ulcer. What is the recommended syphilis testing algorithm per CDC 2024?

options.text: Reverse algorithm: Treponemal EIA/CIA first; if positive, reflex to RPR/VDRL titer

explanation.whyCorrect: CDC 2024: Reverse sequence algorithm. Start with TREPONEMAL test (EIA/CIA) which detects antibodies to T. pallidum. If positive → reflex to NON-treponemal test (RPR/VDRL) for quantitative titer. If discordant (EIA+, RPR-), perform second treponemal test (TPPA). Traditional algorithm starts with RPR first. Reverse algorithm is more sensitive for early and late syphilis.

whyWrong.A: Dark-field microscopy is rarely available; not standard first-line.
whyWrong.B: Reverse algorithm = Treponemal EIA → RPR titer. Standard in US labs.
whyWrong.C: RPR alone misses very early or old treated syphilis.
whyWrong.D: FTA-ABS is confirmatory treponemal test, not initial screen.
whyWrong.E: Clinical diagnosis alone insufficient (many are asymptomatic).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Syphilis testing (2024): Reverse algorithm - Treponemal EIA first → RPR titer. Treatment: Penicillin G benzathine 2.4M units IM x1 (early).

vignette: A 40-year-old male from Eastern Europe has pulmonary TB with resistance to rifampin and isoniazid (MDR-TB). What is the recommended regimen per WHO 2024?

options.text: BPaLM regimen: Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin (6 months, all-oral)

explanation.whyCorrect: WHO 2024: BPaLM regimen (6 months, ALL-ORAL) is now recommended for MDR-TB (including fluoroquinolone-resistant). ZeNix and TB-PRACTECAL trials showed >90% cure with 6-month all-oral regimen vs 50-60% with 18-24 month injectable regimens. Bedaquiline (diarylquinoline), Pretomanid (nitroimidazole), Linezolid (oxazolidinone), Moxifloxacin (if susceptible). No injectables needed! Shorter, safer, more effective.

whyWrong.A: RIPE ineffective for MDR-TB (resistant to rifampin and INH).
whyWrong.B: BPaLM: 6-month all-oral regimen. >90% cure. Game-changer for MDR-TB.
whyWrong.C: Injectable regimens are obsolete (toxic, long, less effective).
whyWrong.D: Standard first-line ineffective for MDR.
whyWrong.E: MDR-TB is curable with appropriate therapy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: MDR-TB (2024): BPaLM = 6-month all-oral regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid, moxifloxacin). >90% cure. No injectables!

vignette: A 30-year-old traveler returns from Thailand with high fever, severe myalgia ('breakbone fever'), retro-orbital headache, and petechiae. Platelets are 60K. What is the MOST important management?

options.text: Acetaminophen for fever + IV fluids + Monitor for warning signs (hemoconcentration, hypotension)

explanation.whyCorrect: Dengue fever: Supportive care is key. Acetaminophen for fever/pain (AVOID NSAIDs and aspirin - increase bleeding risk from thrombocytopenia and platelet dysfunction). IV fluids for dehydration or warning signs (hemoconcentration ≥20%, hypotension, narrow pulse pressure). Monitor for plasma leakage (critical phase day 3-7). Platelet transfusion ONLY if active bleeding + Plt <20K or <50K with surgery. No antiviral approved.

whyWrong.A: Aspirin increases bleeding risk in dengue (antiplatelet effect).
whyWrong.B: Acetaminophen + IV fluids + Close monitoring. Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin.
whyWrong.C: NSAIDs increase bleeding risk.
whyWrong.D: Prophylactic platelet transfusion NOT recommended (no benefit, fluid overload risk).
whyWrong.E: No antiviral therapy proven for dengue.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Dengue: Acetaminophen + Fluids. AVOID NSAIDs/aspirin. Monitor for plasma leakage (critical phase day 3-7). Platelets only for active bleeding.

vignette: A 28-year-old MSM with inconsistent condom use requests HIV PrEP. He prefers non-daily option. What is the FDA-approved long-acting PrEP?

explanation.whyCorrect: Cabotegravir LA (Apretude) is the FIRST long-acting injectable PrEP. HPTN 083 (MSM) and HPTN 084 (cisgender women) showed superior efficacy to daily oral TDF/FTC. IM injection every 2 months after initial 2 monthly loading doses. Ideal for patients with adherence challenges or preference for non-daily option. Daily oral PrEP remains effective but requires daily adherence.

whyWrong.A: Daily oral TDF/FTC is effective but requires daily pill.
whyWrong.B: Cabotegravir LA: IM q2mo. Superior to daily oral TDF/FTC. Long-acting.
whyWrong.C: Descovy is daily oral PrEP (not approved for receptive vaginal sex).
whyWrong.D: On-demand 2-1-1 is for MSM only; not FDA-approved but supported by IPERGAY.
whyWrong.E: Dapivirine ring is for cisgender women in Africa (not US-approved).

explanation.clinicalPearl: LA-PrEP: Cabotegravir (Apretude) IM q2mo. HPTN 083/084: Superior to daily oral PrEP. Game-changer for adherence.

vignette: A 55-year-old diabetic male presents with severe pain out of proportion to exam, rapidly progressing erythema, crepitus on palpation, and hemodynamic instability in his left leg. What is the BEST management?

options.text: EMERGENT radical surgical debridement + Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Vancomycin + Pip-Tazo + Clindamycin)
CT scan first, then decide on surgery

explanation.whyCorrect: Necrotizing fasciitis (Type I polymicrobial): SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Immediate radical debridement of all necrotic tissue. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics: Vancomycin (MRSA) + Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Carbapenem (gram-negatives/anaerobes) + Clindamycin (inhibits toxin production). Clindamycin is critical for its antitoxin effect (inhibits M protein in GAS). Hyperbaric O2 is ADJUNCTIVE after surgery, not primary.

whyWrong.A: Antibiotics alone without surgical debridement = 100% mortality.
whyWrong.B: EMERGENT surgery + Broad-spectrum antibiotics (including clindamycin for antitoxin).
whyWrong.C: Hyperbaric O2 is adjunctive after surgery; not primary therapy.
whyWrong.D: CT may delay life-saving surgery. Clinical diagnosis sufficient.
whyWrong.E: Bedside I&D inadequate; needs radical OR debridement.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Necrotizing fasciitis: EMERGENT radical debridement. Add clindamycin (antitoxin effect). Do NOT delay surgery for imaging.

references: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Guidelines 2023

vignette: A 60-year-old male from Southeast Asia with rheumatoid arthritis is starting prednisone 40mg daily. He has eosinophilia (eosinophils 1200) and intermittent abdominal pain. What is the MOST important screening before immunosuppression?

options.text: Strongyloides serology + Treat with ivermectin if positive BEFORE steroids

explanation.whyCorrect: Strongyloides stercoralis: Endemic in tropical/subtropical regions. Can persist decades via autoinfection. Steroids and other immunosuppression can cause HYPERINFECTION SYNDROME (disseminated strongyloidiasis) with >60% mortality. ALL patients from endemic areas should be screened with serology (ELISA) before immunosuppression. Treat with Ivermectin 200mcg/kg x2 days if positive. Stool O&P has low sensitivity (50%).

whyWrong.A: Strongyloides hyperinfection is a medical emergency with high mortality.
whyWrong.B: Screen endemic patients with serology BEFORE steroids. Ivermectin treatment if positive.
whyWrong.C: Stool O&P has low sensitivity (50%); serology is preferred.
whyWrong.D: Empiric ivermectin (not albendazole) is alternative to screening in high-risk patients.
whyWrong.E: CT is not screening; may show duodenal thickening in chronic infection.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Strongyloides + Steroids = HYPERINFECTION SYNDROME (mortality >60%). Screen ALL patients from endemic areas with serology before immunosuppression.

vignette: A 70-year-old male 3 months post-total knee arthroplasty presents with persistent knee pain, swelling, and sinus tract draining purulent fluid. ESR is 85, CRP is 60. What is the BEST management?

options.text: IV antibiotics for 6 weeks without surgery
Two-stage revision arthroplasty (remove prosthesis, antibiotic spacer, IV antibiotics 6 weeks, then reimplant)

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic PJI (>30 days post-op) with sinus tract: Two-stage exchange is the gold standard. Stage 1: Remove prosthesis, debride, place antibiotic-loaded cement spacer, IV antibiotics 6 weeks (tailored to cultures). Stage 2: Reimplant new prosthesis when infection cleared (normal CRP/ESR). One-stage revision is for selected patients (healthy, known organism, no sinus tract). Irrigation and debridement is for acute PJI (<30 days) with well-fixed prosthesis.

whyWrong.A: Antibiotics alone cannot clear biofilm on prosthesis.
whyWrong.B: Two-stage revision = Gold standard for chronic PJI.
whyWrong.C: One-stage is for selected patients without sinus tract.
whyWrong.D: Arthroscopic washout insufficient for chronic PJI.
whyWrong.E: Suppression alone is for non-surgical candidates (palliative).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chronic PJI with sinus tract: Two-stage revision (remove → spacer → IV antibiotics → reimplant). Biofilm on prosthesis = must remove.

vignette: A 40-year-old male with AIDS (CD4 30, not on ART) presents with headache, fever, and confusion for 2 weeks. CSF shows encapsulated yeast on India ink, opening pressure 35 cmH2O. What is the BEST management?

options.text: Amphotericin B + Flucytosine x2 weeks (induction) → Fluconazole x8 weeks (consolidation) + Therapeutic LPs for elevated opening pressure

explanation.whyCorrect: Cryptococcal meningitis in AIDS: Induction: Amphotericin B + Flucytosine x2 weeks (superior to AmB alone per ACTA trial). Consolidation: Fluconazole 400-800mg x8 weeks. Maintenance: Fluconazole 200mg until CD4 >100 on ART for >3 months. ELEVATED OPENING PRESSURE: Therapeutic LPs (remove 20-30mL CSF) daily or as needed to reduce ICP. Voriconazole and echinocandins have no activity against Cryptococcus.

whyWrong.A: Fluconazole alone is inferior for induction (higher mortality).
whyWrong.B: AmB + Flucytosine induction (2 weeks) → Fluconazole consolidation. Therapeutic LPs for elevated ICP.
whyWrong.C: AmB alone inferior to AmB + Flucytosine.
whyWrong.D: Voriconazole has no activity against Cryptococcus.
whyWrong.E: Echinocandins have NO activity against Cryptococcus.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Cryptococcal meningitis: AmB + Flucytosine x2wk → Fluconazole. Therapeutic LPs for elevated ICP (>25 cmH2O). Start ART after 4-6 weeks.

vignette: A 45-year-old female with breast cancer on chemotherapy presents with fever 38.3°C and ANC 400. She is hemodynamically stable, has no localizing symptoms, and is reliable with 24/7 caregiver access. What is the BEST management per MASCC criteria?

options.text: Outpatient management with oral ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate (if low-risk per MASCC score >21)

explanation.whyCorrect: Low-risk febrile neutropenia (MASCC score >21, ANC >100, expected neutropenia <7 days, no organ failure, outpatient feasible): Oral ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate (or levofloxacin) is non-inferior to IV antibiotics. Requires: Reliable patient, 24/7 caregiver, close to hospital, telephone follow-up. High-risk (MASCC <21, ANC <100, prolonged neutropenia, comorbidities): Admit for IV cefepime.

whyWrong.A: Admission is for HIGH-risk febrile neutropenia per MASCC.
whyWrong.B: Low-risk: Outpatient oral antibiotics. MASCC score guides risk stratification.
whyWrong.C: Febrile neutropenia always requires empiric antibiotics (risk of rapid deterioration).
whyWrong.D: Home IV antibiotics are alternative but oral is preferred for low-risk.
whyWrong.E: G-CSF alone does not treat established febrile neutropenia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Febrile neutropenia: MASCC score >21 = Low-risk = Outpatient oral antibiotics. MASCC <21 = Admit for IV antibiotics.

vignette: A 55-year-old male in Connecticut presents with fever, headache, and a maculopapular rash 5 days after a tick bite. Labs show leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated LFTs. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: HGA (Anaplasma phagocytophilum): Fever + Headache + Maculopapular rash + Leukopenia + Thrombocytopenia + Elevated LFTs. Tick bite (Ixodes, same vector as Lyme). Blood smear shows MORTULAE (intracytoplasmic inclusions in neutrophils). PCR confirms. Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BID x7-10 days (same for all tick-borne rickettsial diseases). Lyme rarely causes leukopenia/thrombocytopenia. Babesiosis causes hemolytic anemia. RMSF has petechial rash on palms/soles.

whyWrong.A: Lyme: Erythema migrans, arthritis, no leukopenia/thrombocytopenia.
whyWrong.B: HGA: Leukopenia + Thrombocytopenia + Elevated LFTs + Mortulae. Doxycycline.
whyWrong.C: Babesiosis: Hemolytic anemia, no rash, intraerythrocytic parasites.
whyWrong.D: RMSF: Petechial rash starting on wrists/ankles spreading centrally.
whyWrong.E: Tularemia: Ulceroglandular, not tick-borne with pancytopenia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Tick-borne + Fever + Leukopenia + Thrombocytopenia + LFTs = Anaplasmosis/Ehrlichiosis. Doxycycline! Mortulae on blood smear.

vignette: A 50-year-old CMV D+/R- kidney transplant recipient (highest risk) is 1 month post-transplant on tacrolimus/MMF/prednisone. What is the recommended prevention strategy?

options.text: No prophylaxis, monitor CMV PCR weekly
Valganciclovir 900mg daily x3-6 months (prophylaxis) OR Letermovir (for kidney transplant)
Ganciclovir only if CMV PCR positive

explanation.whyCorrect: High-risk CMV (D+/R-): Universal PROPHYLAXIS with valganciclovir 900mg daily x3-6 months. Letermovir (CMV terminase inhibitor) is FDA-approved for kidney transplant (2023), less myelotoxic than valganciclovir. Preemptive therapy (monitor PCR weekly, treat if positive) is for INTERMEDIATE-RISK (R+). Acyclovir has NO activity against CMV. Foscarnet is for ganciclovir-resistant CMV.

whyWrong.A: High-risk requires universal prophylaxis, not preemptive therapy.
whyWrong.B: Valganciclovir x3-6mo or Letermovir. Prophylaxis for D+/R-.
whyWrong.C: Acyclovir is inactive against CMV (requires ganciclovir/valganciclovir).
whyWrong.D: Preemptive strategy is for intermediate-risk (R+).
whyWrong.E: Foscarnet is for resistant CMV (second-line).

explanation.clinicalPearl: CMV D+/R- transplant: Valganciclovir prophylaxis x3-6mo. Letermovir (new, less myelotoxic). Preemptive for R+ only.

vignette: A 65-year-old diabetic male presents with 4 weeks of progressive back pain, fever, and elevated ESR/CRP. MRI shows T11-T12 discitis and vertebral body enhancement. Blood cultures grew S. aureus. What is the BEST management?

options.text: IV antibiotics for 6 weeks (targeted to cultures) + Pain control + Bracing
Emergency surgical debridement for all patients

explanation.whyCorrect: Vertebral osteomyelitis with positive blood cultures: Targeted IV antibiotics for 6 weeks (S. aureus = nafcillin or cefazolin for MSSA, vancomycin for MRSA). Blood cultures identify organism in 50-70% (bone biopsy not needed if positive). Surgery indications: Neurologic deficit, spinal instability, epidural abscess, or failed medical therapy. Bracing for pain/instability. Oral step-down after IV if improving.

whyWrong.A: 2 weeks insufficient for vertebral osteomyelitis (minimum 6 weeks).
whyWrong.B: 6 weeks IV antibiotics = Standard. Surgery for complications.
whyWrong.C: Surgery only for specific indications (neurologic deficit, abscess, instability).
whyWrong.D: Bone biopsy indicated if blood cultures negative. Positive BC = no biopsy needed.
whyWrong.E: Oral antibiotics alone insufficient for initial treatment (start IV).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Vertebral osteomyelitis: IV antibiotics x6 weeks. Blood cultures often identify organism (no biopsy needed). Surgery for neurologic deficit.

vignette: A hospital implements an antibiotic stewardship program. Which intervention has the STRONGEST evidence for reducing C. difficile rates and antibiotic resistance?

options.text: Formulary restriction of all broad-spectrum antibiotics
Prospective audit and feedback + Preauthorization of restricted antibiotics + De-escalation prompts at 48-72 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: CDC Core Elements 2023: Prospective audit and feedback (pharmacist reviews antibiotics daily, provides feedback to prescriber) + Preauthorization (requires approval for restricted antibiotics) + De-escalation prompts (stop or narrow antibiotics at 48-72h based on cultures). This bundled approach reduces antibiotic use by 20-30%, C. difficile by 30-50%, and resistance rates. Education alone is insufficient.

whyWrong.A: Complete formulary restriction is impractical and may harm patient care.
whyWrong.B: Audit + Feedback + Preauthorization = Most effective stewardship interventions.
whyWrong.C: Antibiotic cycling has limited evidence and is less effective than audit/feedback.
whyWrong.D: Education alone has minimal and unsustained impact.
whyWrong.E: Guidelines without active stewardship have limited impact.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Antibiotic stewardship: Prospective audit + Feedback + Preauthorization = Most effective. De-escalation at 48-72h. Reduces C. diff and resistance.

references: CDC Core Elements of Antibiotic Stewardship 2023

vignette: A 72-year-old female with COPD asks about RSV vaccination. What is the recommendation per CDC 2024?

options.text: RSV vaccine not indicated for adults
Single dose of RSV vaccine (RSVpreF - Arexvy or Abrysvo) for adults ≥60 years (shared decision-making)

explanation.whyCorrect: CDC 2024: RSV vaccination recommended for adults ≥60 years using SHARED DECISION-MAKING (consider patient's risk and preference). Two vaccines approved (2023): RSVpreF (Arexvy - GSK) and RSVpreF (Abrysvo - Pfizer). Both are single-dose, non-live, protein subunit vaccines. Abrysvo also approved for pregnancy (32-36 weeks) to protect infants. Efficacy: 80-85% against severe RSV disease in older adults.

whyWrong.A: RSV vaccines are now FDA-approved and CDC-recommended for ≥60.
whyWrong.B: Single dose RSV vaccine. Shared decision-making. Not yet annual.
whyWrong.C: Not yet determined if annual dosing needed (durability studies ongoing).
whyWrong.D: RSV vaccine is for ALL ≥60, not just immunocompromised.
whyWrong.E: Ribavirin is treatment, not prophylaxis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: RSV vaccine (2024): Single dose for ≥60 (shared decision-making). Arexvy or Abrysvo. 80-85% efficacy. Also for pregnant women (Abrysvo).

vignette: A 40-year-old female from rural Brazil has positive Trypanosoma cruzi serology. ECG shows RBBB and left anterior fascicular block. Echo shows apical aneurysm. What is the BEST management?

options.text: Benznidazole OR Nifurtimox (antitrypanosomal therapy) + Cardiac management (ICD if EF <35% or VT)

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy: Antitrypanosomal therapy (benznidazole or nifurtimox) is recommended for patients <50-60 years without advanced cardiomyopathy. BENEFIT trial: Benznidazole reduced parasitemia but did not significantly reduce clinical cardiac outcomes (though trend toward benefit). ICD for EF <35% or sustained VT (Chagas is arrhythmogenic). Anticoagulation for apical aneurysm/thrombus. Heart transplant for end-stage.

whyWrong.A: Antitrypanosomal therapy recommended for eligible patients.
whyWrong.B: Benznidazole/Nifurtimox + Cardiac management (ICD if indicated).
whyWrong.C: Transplant only for end-stage heart failure.
whyWrong.D: Amiodarone for VT; does not treat underlying infection.
whyWrong.E: Prednisone has no role in Chagas disease.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chagas cardiomyopathy: RBBB + LAFB + Apical aneurysm. Benznidazole for eligible patients. ICD for EF <35% or VT.
